coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots induce endoplasmic reticulum stress-mediated lipid dysregulation and embryonic developmental toxicity in zebrafish
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Inc.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Inc.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhong, Xiaoyan
Zeng, Liwen
Cai, Yunnuo
Zhu, Ying
Ma, Qiyao
Shen, Ouxi
Song, Xiaoyao
Zhang, Jie
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) are widely utilized due to their exceptional physical and chemical properties. Nevertheless, there is a paucity of research examining the potential toxicity of carbon dots to human health, particularly with regard to developmental toxicity. The present study demonstrated that exposure to CDs resulted in increased mortality and malformations in zebrafish embryos. Further bioinformatics analyses indicated that CDs-induced lipid metabolism disorders may represent a significant pathway for developmental toxicity in zebrafish embryos. This can result in aberrant expression of genes involved in lipid metabolism, which ultimately leads to endoplasmic reticulum stress (ERS)-induced accumulation of excess lipids in the body. It can therefore be surmised that exposure to CDs in early life ultimately leads to developmental toxicity by inducing ERS-induced lipid metabolism disorders. The findings of this study suggest that there is a risk of long-term exposure to CDs from early life, and provide a theoretical basis and data support for the prevention of potential hazards of CDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Developmental toxicity
Transcriptomics
Lipid metabolism disorders
Endoplasmic reticulum stress

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and characterization of carbon dots (CDs)
Biodistribution of carbon dots in zebrafish
Effect on embryo development of carbon points
Spontaneous locomotor capacity of CDs-exposed zebrafish
Real-time reverse transcription-polymerase chain reaction (RT-PCR) assay
The observation of the effect on endoplasmic reticulum (ER)
Effects of CDs on developmental toxicity in zebrafish embryos
The impact of CDs on lipid metabolism in zebrafish embryos
Effects of CDs on endoplasmic reticulum stress in zebrafish embryos
The CDs induce developmental toxicity in zebrafish embryos through the induction of endoplasmic reticulum stress
The CDs induce lipid metabolism disorder of zebrafish embryos through endoplasmic reticulum stress

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The study was supported by the Undergraduate Training for Program Innovation and Entrepreneurship, Soochow University (No. 202310285071Z) and the Opening Fund of NHC Key Laboratory of Chronobiology (Sichuan) (No. NHCC-2024-02).The study was also supported by the Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Preventive and Translational Medicine for Geriatric Diseases, MOE Key Laboratory of Geriatric Diseases and Immunology; Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (PAPD) and Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Preventive and Translational Medicine for Major Chronic Non-communicable Diseases

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The effect of CDs on the developmental toxicity and spontaneous motor behavior of zebrafish. (A) The bright field images of zebrafish showing CDs (260 μg/mL) induced malformation at 96 hpf. (B) The malformation rates of zebrafish embryos after exposure to different concentrations of CDs at 96 hpf. (C) The body lengths of zebrafish embryos after exposure to different concentrations of CDs at 72, 96 hpf, respectively. (D) The mortality rates of zebrafish embryos that were exposed to different concentrations of CDs at 24, 48, 72, and 96 hpf, respectively. (E) The movement distance of zebrafish with light and dim light irradiation every 20 minutes after exposure to different concentrations of CDs for 96 h. (F) The cumulative distance of swimming corresponding to every 20 minutes irradiation with light and dim in figure (E). Data are presented as mean ± SD. *, P < 0.05, **, P < 0.01. Abbreviations: yolk-sac edema (YSE), pericardial edema (PE), and spinal curvature (SC).

The GO and KEGG enrichment analysis of DEGs. (A) The GO analysis of the functional categories of the DEGs. (B) The KEGG pathway analysis of the top 20 enriched pathways with the most significant P value. The X-axis indicated the percentages of DEGs belonging to the corresponding pathways. The left Y-axis showed the top 20 pathways. The sizes of bubble represented the numbers of DEGs in the corresponding pathways, and the colors of the bubble represented the enrichment P value of the corresponding pathway. (C) The KEGG pathway analysis. (D) The heat maps of the DEGs of three GO terms. Group a1-a3 represented the three samples in the control group with no CDs treatment. Group b1-b3 represented the three samples were treated with 260 μg/mL of CDs.

The expression of genes related to lipid metabolism and the endoplasmic reticulum in zebrafish after exposure to different concentrations of CDs to 96 hpf. (A-C) The highly expressed genes related to lipid metabolism. (D-F) The lowly expressed genes related to lipid metabolism. (G-I) The highly expressed genes related to endoplasmic reticulum stress. *, P < 0.05; **, P < 0.01.

Lipid changes in the zebrafish embryos after different concentrations of CDs treatment to 96 hpf. (A) The digital images showed the lipid distribution of zebrafish embryos stained by Oil-Red-O (260 μg/mL). The black arrow indicates the point of colour change in the same location in comparison to the control group. (B) The quantification result of lipid Oil-Red-O. (C-F) The alterations of lipid content including TG (C), TCHO (D), EPA (E), and DHA (F) in zebrafish embryos. All data are presented as mean ± SEM. *, P < 0.05; **, P < 0.01.
Expression of endoplasmic reticulum stress-related genes of zebrafish embryos. All data are presented as mean ± SEM. *, P < 0.05; **, P < 0.01; ***, P < 0.001; ****, P < 0.0001.

Effects of 4-PBA and CDs on growth and development of zebrafish embryos. All data are presented as mean ± SEM. (A) The mortality rate of zebrafish embryos. (B) The hatching rate of zebrafish embryos. (C) The flow activity of zebrafish embryos. (D) The heart rate of zebrafish embryos. (E) The heart malformation rate of zebrafish embryos. (F) The SV-BA distance of zebrafish embryos. All data were presented as mean ± SEM. * represents significant difference compared with control group，*, P < 0.05; **, P < 0.01; ****, P < 0.0001; # represents significant difference compared with CDs group，#, P < 0.05; ####, P < 0.0001.

Effects of 4-PBA on CDs exposure-induced ER stress and lipid metabolism disorder of zebrafish embryos. (A) The contents of TG. (B) The contents of TCHO. (C-I) The expression levels of ER stress-related genes. All data are presented as mean ± SEM. * represents significant difference compared with control group，*P < 0.05, **, P < 0.01, ***, P < 0.001，****, P < 0.0001. # represents significant difference compared with CDs group，#，P < 0.05, ##，P < 0.01, ###，P < 0.001，####, P < 0.0001.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots induce endoplasmic reticulum stress-mediated lipid dysregulation and embryonic developmental toxicity in zebrafish

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Toxicology, School of Public Health, Medical College of Soochow University, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Toxicology, School of Public Health, Medical College of Soochow University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Toxicology, School of Public Health, Medical College of Soochow University, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Suzhou Industrial Park Disease Control Centre Co, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Suzhou Industrial Park Disease Control Centre Co

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Suzhou Industrial Park Disease Control Centre Co

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Xiaoyan Zhong, Liwen Zeng and Yunnuo Cai contributed equally to this paper

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) are widely utilized due to their exceptional physical and chemical properties. Nevertheless, there is a paucity of research examining the potential toxicity of carbon dots to human health, particularly with regard to developmental toxicity. The present study demonstrated that exposure to CDs resulted in increased mortality and malformations in zebrafish embryos. Further bioinformatics analyses indicated that CDs-induced lipid metabolism disorders may represent a significant pathway for developmental toxicity in zebrafish embryos. This can result in aberrant expression of genes involved in lipid metabolism, which ultimately leads to endoplasmic reticulum stress (ERS)-induced accumulation of excess lipids in the body. It can therefore be surmised that exposure to CDs in early life ultimately leads to developmental toxicity by inducing ERS-induced lipid metabolism disorders. The findings of this study suggest that there is a risk of long-term exposure to CDs from early life, and provide a theoretical basis and data support for the prevention of potential hazards of CDs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are nanoparticles derived from a carbon source. They are spherical fluorescent carbon particles less than 10 nanometres in size(Xu et al., 2004), which were discovered by arc discharge synthesis (Li et al., 2021; Jaleel and Pramod, 2018). In recent years, carbon-based nanomaterials have received increasing attention due to their superior physicochemical properties (Peng et al., 2020). CDs have been widely applied in the fields of biomedicine, agriculture, the electronics industry, and environmental pollution control (Miao et al., 2015). The carbon dots have the potential to cause serious cytotoxicity and interfere with organ development (Roy et al., 2015). Dietary carbon dots exposure induces insulin resistance via gut flora dysbiosis and damage to the intestinal mucosa (Zhang et al., 2023). Additionally, CDs have the potential to penetrate the nucleus, interact with genetic material, and induce DNA damage and G0/G1 phase arrest in cancer cells (Şimşek et al., 2020). Furthermore, it has been reported that pericardial and yolk sac edema, delayed growth, spinal cord flexure, and death were observed in zebrafish embryos at CD concentrations > 200 μg/mL (Liu et al., 2020a). The high CD concentrations were further associated with the reduction in zebrafish larval locomotor activity and decreased dopamine levels, reduced frequencies of tyrosine hydroxylase-positive dopaminergic neurons, and multiple organ damage (Liu et al., 2020b). In addition, CDs exposure also affects the immune function of healthy BALB/c mice by inducing Th1 and Tc responses(Gao et al., 2013). While numerous aspects of CDs have been examined in the afore-mentioned studies, research on their developmental toxicity remains scarce. To this end, the zebrafish has been selected as a model organism (Liu et al., 2020a). In light of the findings, the present study aims to elucidate the underlying mechanisms through which CDs exert their effects on embryonic and juvenile development following exposure during the embryonic stage.

It is known that lipids are a significant source of energy for the formation and hatching of zebrafish embryos during the early stages of development (Dutta and Sinha, 2017; Fraher et al., 2016). Therefore, any disruption to lipid metabolism may result in growth retardation and the development of malformations in the embryos (Thangarajh et al., 2019; Moran, 2007a). These malformations can include yolk sac malformation and pericardial edema(Teng et al., 2019a), as well as potentially leading to systemic metabolic disorders (Du et al., 2021). Luo et al. found the utilisation of yolk lipids for energy metabolism in zebrafish embryos exposed to PFOS (Perfluorooctane Sulphonate) was found to be impaired (Yang et al., 2023b). Previous study examines the influence of diverse fat types on children's cognitive growth, emphasizing the pivotal function of essential fatty acids in brain performance. The findings indicate that a well-balanced intake of these fats is linked to enhanced cognitive outcomes, including memory and learning capabilities (Jumbe et al., 2016b; Jumbe et al., 2016a; Adjepong et al., 2018). A study examined the influence of fatty acids on cognitive performance, with findings indicating that a well-balanced intake of essential fatty acids is linked to enhanced cognitive functions, including memory, attention, and problem-solving abilities (Capitao et al., 2017). Furthermore, environmental antifouling agents have been shown to lead to fish toxicity and liver lipid accumulation by inducing endoplasmic reticulum stress (Kang and Lee, 2019).

In this study, we have conducted a systematic investigation into the effects of exposure to different concentrations of CDs (0, 60, 130, 260 μg/mL) on the developmental toxicity of zebrafish embryos. The changes of spontaneous behavioral activities during the developmental process were observed, and changes in metrics and gene expression associated with lipid metabolism and endoplasmic reticulum stress were quantified using a transcriptomic approach. The objective of this study is to provide a comprehensive analysis of the potential risks associated with CDs on zebrafish embryos, with a view to enhancing our understanding of the underlying mechanisms. This will facilitate the exploration of the effects of CDs on human and even ecological health, and provide new perspectives and data to support the study of CDs toxicity.

The graphite rod (99.99 %) was purchased from Alfa Aesar Co. Ltd. All the other chemicals used were without any purification. 4-phenylbutyric acid (4-PBA) were purchased from Merk (America). Diethyl pyrocarbonate (DEPC) and Trizol were purchased from Beyotime biotechnology (Shanghai, China). 1-PHENYL-2-THIOUREA (C7H8N2S) and CHCl3 were purchased from Sinopharm (China). Oil red O (ORO) were purchased from Sigama-Aldrich (Shanghai, China) and lipid detection kit were purchased from Fusheng (Shanghai, China). Reverse transcription kit were perchased from Roche (Swiss Confederation).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of carbon dots (CDs)
ce:section.ce:para: The CDs was synthetized by electrochemical stripping method according to the previously reported literature (Ming et al., 2012). The detailed protocol for the synthesis and characterization of carbon dots was described in detail in Supplementary Material S1.

All animal procedures were authorized by the Ethics Committee of Soochow University (No. SUDA20230201A01). Wild type zebrafish of the AB strain (length 3.4 ± 0.5 mm; 0.2 ± 0.1 g) were obtained from the Zebrafish Experimental Centre of Soochow University and were maintained in the flow-through feeding tank (Esen Corp, Shanghai, China). All the fish were maintained at 28 °C under a 14–10 light/dark cycle, and received feeding twice daily. Adult male and female fish (male/female ratio was 1/2) were separated overnight by isolation boards in spawning boxes. The next morning, spawning was induced by turning on the light and removing the isolation plates. After washing with tank medium water, the embryos were placed on clean Petri dishes, add embryo medium, CDs, or 4-PBA, and change embryo medium every 24 hours.

CDs concentration: Based on the LC50 of CDs, we select approximately 1/2 of the LC50 as the highest concentration, and then determine the other two concentrations (60, 130 μg/mL) according to the proportionality principle. CDs solution: Take 20 mL of carbon dot solution with a concentration of 18 mg/mL in a 50 mL centrifuge tube, add 20 mL of deionized water to prepare a mother liquor with a concentration of 9 mg/mL, and store it in a 4 °C freezer for later use (wrapped in tin foil and protected from light). 10 mM 4-PBA: Weigh 2.2 mg of 4-PBA, add 1.3398 mL of DMSO to dissolve, and store in a −20 °C freezer for later use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biodistribution of carbon dots in zebrafish
ce:section.ce:para: 30 zebrafish embryos per well of the six-well flat-bottomed cell culture plates and were soaked with 3 mL of the CDs solutions at different concentrations. The solution contains 0.003 wt% PTU to block pigmentation and mediate visualisation. The glass slide was then placed under a fluorescence microscope for fluorescence imaging using a fluorescence microscope (Olympus Corporation, Japan).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect on embryo development of carbon points
ce:section.ce:para: First, 30 embryos at 4 hours post fertilization (hpf) that in each group were incubated with different concentrations (0, 60, 130, and 260 μg/mL) of CDs, respectively. After exposure, the development of the zebrafish embryos was monitored under a microscope. Zebrafish embryo survival was assessed at 24, 48, 72, and 96 hpf. Hatching rate was only examined at 72 hpf. In addition, the body length was assessed at both 72 and 96 hpf, whereas the malformation rate was assessed at 96 hpf only. The length of each embryo was measured from the most anterior part of the head to the tip of the tail by using digital images acquired with Image-Pro Plus (Media Cybernetics, MD, U.S.). Subsequently, following the introduction of 4-PBA (10 μM) between 4 and 96 hpf, the body length of each group was determined under a microscope at 72 hpf. Additionally, the incidence of malformations, mortality, hatching and cardiac malformations was observed at 96 hpf. The SV-BA distance is defined as the distance (in micrometres) between the cardiac sinus venous (SV) and bulbus arteriosus (BA) regions of the heart. The SV-BA distance can be used to reflect the positional changes of the atria and ventricles in cases where the heart is affected. This is due to fact that the position of the atria and ventricles will be changed, and the SV-BA distance will be changed accordingly (Bambino and Chu, 2017) Following a 72-hour exposure to PRX, the distance between cardiac SV-BAs was quantified. The findings revealed a significant increase in SV-BA distances in the 20 mg/L PRX-exposed group relative to the control group (Zhu et al., 2023). By measuring the SV-BA distance, it is possible to judge the effect of the test compounds on the heart. The distance was measured at 96 hpf, with images captured to ensure consistency in camera settings. The heartbeat and blood flow were documented for a period of 30 seconds for 30 s.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Spontaneous locomotor capacity of CDs-exposed zebrafish
ce:section.ce:para: Motor behavior of zebrafish is a marker for evaluating neurodevelopmental toxicology of toxicants (Boakye-Yiadom et al., 2019). Briefly, after exposure at 120 hpf, each embryo was placed in a 96-well plate (12 embryos in each group). The embryos were then exposed to visible light for 20 min, followed by a further 20 min in the dark. During the alternation between light and dark for a total of 120 min, the 96-well plates were placed in the Zebrabox/DanioVision observation chamber at 28.5 °C. Swimming activity was then recorded using a video tracking system (Videotrack, Viewpoint Life Sciences, Noldus Information Technology), and further analyzed using Zebralab 3.10 software (Viewpoint Life Sciences, Lyon, France).

Total RNA from zebrafish embryos samples was extracted with Trizol according to the manufacturer's protocol (Invitrogen, Carlsbad, CA, U.S.). The concentration of total RNA in each sample was determined using a Qubit® RNA Assay Kit and a Qubit® 2.0 Flurometer (Life Technologies, CA, U.S.). An aliquot of 3 μg of RNA was used to construct libraries by using the NEBNext® Ultra™ RNA LibraryPrep Kit for Illumina® (NEB, MA, U.S.). After clustering, the libraries were sequenced on an Illumina Hiseq 2000 platform to generate 100 bp paired-end reads.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Real-time reverse transcription-polymerase chain reaction (RT-PCR) assay
ce:section.ce:para: Thirty of embryos from each treatment group were collected at 96 hpf in each replicate experiment (three replicates in total). Total RNA was extracted using Trizol reagent according to the manufacturer's protocol and was determined using a NanoDrop 2000 spectrophotometer (NanoDrop Technology, DE, U.S.). cDNA was synthesised by RT reactions using a reverse transcriptase kit according to the manufacturer's instructions. Quantitative real-time PCR amplifications were performed on an ABI 7500 real-time-PCR system (Applied Biosystems, CA, U.S.) using the SYBR Green PCR Master Mix reagent kits (Vazyme, Nanjing, China). The thermal cycle was as follows: denaturation for 2 min at 50 °C, and 10 min at 95 °C, followed by 40 cycles at 95 °C for 15 s, annealing at 60 °C for 60 s. Gene expression levels were measured in triplicate for each treatment. The sequences of the primers used in this study were shown in Table S2. The fold change was calculated using the 2-△△CT method.

The extraction and measurement of triglycerides (TG), total cholesterol (TCHO), eicosapentaenoic acid (EPA) and docosahexaenoic acid (DHA) were investigated. Briefly, approximately 50 zebrafish embryos were randomly selected from each 6-well plate (n=3), homogenised with cell lysates and centrifuged at 3000 ×g for 10 min at 4 °C. The supernatant was transferred and the levels of TG, TCHO, DHA and EPA were measured using an assay kit according to the manufacturer's instructions (Affandi, China). Besides, Oil-Red-O (ORO) staining was also used to detect lipid distribution. Embryos were fixed in 4 % paraformaldehyde (PFA) overnight at 4 °C and then washed with 60 % isopropanol for 1 hour. After incubation with 1 mL ORO working solution (0.25 % ORO stock in 60 % isopropanol) for 15 min, the embryos were washed twice with 60 % isopropanol and photographed under a Nikon SMZ1500 zoom stereomicroscope (Nikon Corporation, Tokyo, Japan).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The observation of the effect on endoplasmic reticulum (ER)
ce:section.ce:para: The ultrastructural observation of ER in CDs-treated zebrafish embryos was performed by TEM imaging. Briefly, zebrafish embryos were incubated with 260 μg/mL of CDs for 96 h. After that, the zebrafish embryos were fixed in stationary liquid (PBS of 0.1 M, 2.5 % glutaraldehyde), embedded in 2 % agarose gel, post-fixed in 4 % osmium tetroxide solution for 1 h, stained with 0.5 % uranyl acetate for 1 h, and dehydrated in a graded series of ethanol. After these treatments, the samples were embedded in epoxy resin and cut into ultrathin sections for TEM observation.

Statistical analysis was performed using SPSS 18.0 (SPSS, Chicago, IL, U.S.). Differences in data were assessed by one-way ANOVA with post-hoc least significant difference (LSD) comparison of means. Data are expressed as mean ± SD. P < 0.05 was considered statistically significant. Heart rate and blood flow velocity were quantified using DanioScope and SPSS 22.0. Raw reads containing adaptor, poly-N and more than 50 % low quality bases were removed to generate approximately 8.4 Gbps of clean bases for each replicate (n=3), which were then aligned to the zebrafish reference genome using Top Hat v2.0.9. Read counts per transcript were determined using HTSeq 0.6.1 and gene transcript levels were expressed as reads per kilobase of exon model per million mapped reads (RPKM). The threshold for differentially expressed genes (DEGs) was set at P ˂ 0.05 and |log2(fold change)| > 0.58. Hierarchical clustering of differential transcripts was performed using Spearman rank correlations and centroid linkages to construct the similarity metric and gene clustering using Gene Cluster 3.0 software, respectively. Gene function annotation was performed using Gene Otology (GO) terminology (http://www.geneontology.org), covering three biological levels (i.e. biological process, cellular component and molecular function). The gene list of each subgroup was submitted to the KOBAS 3.0 web server to determine the enrichment of Kyoto Encyclopedia of Genes and Genomes (KEGG) pathways. In addition, Gene Set Enrichment Analysis (GSEA) was performed to examine pathway enrichment between NIC and control groups, with a false discovery rate (FDR) < 5 % cutoff and P < 0.05.

In this study, CDs were synthesised by an electrochemical stripping method (Ming et al., 2012) and systematically characterized (Supplementary Material S3-S4). The CDs with uniform size, good hydrophilicity and fluorescence properties have been successfully produced, which can be used function as fluorescent probes for in vivo tracking.

After the systemic characterization of CDs, the result of the median lethal concentration (LC50) detection was shown in Supplementary Material S5. Meanwhile, it was found that with the increasing exposure concentrations of CDs and incubation time, fluorescence of CDs was observed inside the zebrafish embryos in the yolk sac, brain, and liver (Supplementary Material S6 A-D). Using intensity optical density (IOD) analysis, quantification of fluorescence intensity further indicated that the uptake of CDs by zebrafish was concentration dependent. The higher concentration of exposed CDs, the higher the fluorescence intensity within the zebrafish body (Supplementary Material S6 E-H). Based on the above results, the CDs were mainly distributed in the yolk sac, suggesting that the accumulation of CDs in zebrafish at the early in the life cycle may have side effects on growth and development.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of CDs on developmental toxicity in zebrafish embryos
ce:section.ce:para: The developmental toxicities induced by CDs were then systematically investigated. Compared to the control group, many abnormal morphologies occurred in the CDs-exposed zebrafish, including pericardial edema (PE), yolk sac edema (YSE) and even spinal curvature (SC) ( Fig. 1A). The malformation rate of zebrafish embryos following exposure to varying concentrations of CDs could be accurately calculated based on the observed deformities. At lower concentrations of CDs exposure (60 μg/mL), the malformation rate of zebrafish embryos was insufficient to be reach statistical significance when compared to the control group. However, when the concentration of CDs was increased to 260 μg/mL, the malformation rate exhibited a notable increase, reaching 16.6 % (Fig. 1B). However, there were no significant alterations in body length were observed at 72 hpf, even at the highest concentration of CDs exposure (260 μg/mL). Conversely, at 96 hpf, after exposure of 130 μg/mL and 260 μg/mL CDs, the body length was 3.57 ± 1.44 mm and 3.35 ± 0.99 mm, respectively, which was significantly shorter than that of the control group (3.76 ± 0.57 mm), and the difference was statistically significant (P < 0.01) (Fig. 1C). The results indicate a notable decline in the body length of zebrafish, particularly in the longer exposure period and the higher exposure group, where the change in body length was most pronounced. Significant differences in mortality rate was observed at 72 and 96 hpf (Fig. 1D). These findings suggest that both malformation and mortality may occur at higher concentrations of CDs exposure (130 and 260 μg/mL). As spontaneous motor behavior is closely linked to structural morphology, the swimming distances of zebrafish exposed to different levels of CDs were monitored in order to gain insight into this relationship. It was evident that, irrespective of light or dark conditions, the real time swimming distance exhibited a gradual decline with increasing concentrations of CDs (Fig. 1E). Furthermore, the analysis of the shortened accumulated swimming distances (Fig. 1F) demonstrated that CDs exposure significantly impaired the spontaneous locomotor ability of zebrafish. The spontaneous locomotor abnormalities were potentially attributable to disrupted neurodevelopment through small molecules, steroid biosynthesis, and oxidative stress.(Thangarajh et al., 2019; Zheng et al., 2021) These findings collectively indicate that a certain level of CDs exposure could precipitate a range of adverse effects on the development of zebrafish.

In order to utilise the genomic data pertaining to the specific biological processes, transcriptome-based methodologies were identify differentially expressed genes (DEGs) in zebrafish following exposure to CDs. A comparison between the control group and the group exposed to 260 μg/mL of CDs revealed a characteristic differential gene profile. In particular, 2271 DEGs were identified in zebrafish embryos following exposure to CDs, comprising 1703 up-regulated DEGs and 568 down-regulated DEGs (Supplementary Material S7).

The DEGs were entered into Gene Ontology (GO) database, and their functions were classified into three categories: biological process, cellular component and molecular function. The DEGs associated with molecular functions were primarily involved in enzymatic activities, including peptidase, endopeptidase, hydrolase, metallopeptidase, and other activities. Those associated with biological processes were mainly involved in proteolysis, immune response, nucleosome assembly, inflammatory response, and lipid metabolism. Some of these were related to cell membrane function and components for cellular components ( Fig. 2A). Further analysis with KEGG revealed that the top 20 significantly enriched pathways included necroptosis, nucleotide oligomerization domain (NOD)-like receptor signalling pathway, apoptosis, cytokine-cytokine receptor interaction, and several others (Fig. 2B). It is noteworthy that eight of these were related to lipid metabolism (e.g. sphingolipid, linolenic/linoleic acid, arachidonic acid, ether lipid and glycerophospholipid) or biosynthesis (e.g. mucin type O glycan and steroid hormone). This finding serves to remind us that CDs-induced developmental toxicity induced by CDs may be strongly associated with lipid metabolism. In view of these findings, our subsequent efforts were concentrated on the DEGs associated with lipid metabolism, and a clustering analysis of the second level of the KEGG was conducted.

The lipid metabolism pathways were directly or indirectly associated with DEGs in the immune, cardiovascular and endocrine systems, as shown in Fig. 2C. This suggests that CDs may exert a deleterious influence on the aforementioned three systems by inducing lipid metabolism disorders. Moreover, the results of the hierarchical clustering analysis of the DEGs indicated that exposure to CDs may influence lipid and fatty acid metabolism, as well as lipid transport processes (Fig. 2D). In particular, a considerable number of genes demonstrated aberrant expression, including members of the apolipoprotein family (apodb, apoeb and apoda2), lipid transporters (abca12), fatty acid elongase (elovl gene family), fatty acid desaturase (fads2), phospholipase A2 (pla2g gene family) and prostaglandin endoperoxide synthase (ptgs gene family). Additionally, the expression of cytochrome P450 (cyp51) and cyp7a1 was observed to be upregulated and downregulated, respectively. All of these genes are involved in a multitude of reactions related to drug metabolism and bile acid synthesis. Given the role of the endoplasmic reticulum (ER) as a primary organelle in lipid anabolism and catabolism (Schwarz and Blower, 2016), it is notable that nine of the DEGs associated with ER function were found to overlap with lipid metabolism (Supplementary Material S8). To validate the accuracy of the transcriptomic data, six DEGs involved in lipid metabolism, including lcat, fads2, pla2g1b, cyp51, cyp7a1, and agpat4, were selected for verification. A correlation analysis was also conducted based on the transcript levels of the DEGs, which demonstrated a strong linear relationship between the RNA-Seq and qRT-PCR results (Supplementary Material S9). The findings indicate that CDs may also exert an influence on ER functionality.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The impact of CDs on lipid metabolism in zebrafish embryos
ce:section.ce:para: A review of the biological information revealed that exposure to CDs may result in the onset of ERS, which in turn disrupts the lipid metabolism process (Geng et al., 2020). The expression of genes involved was detected after zebrafish were exposed to different concentrations of CDs up to 96 hpf. Among the genes involved in lipid metabolism, the expression of cyp51, apgat4 and srebf1 was observed to be up-regulated ( Fig. 3A-C), while the expression of cyp7a1, acox1 and pparab were relatively down-regulated (Fig. 3D-F). Furthermore, the expression of ER-related genes including xbp1s, ern2 and atf4a, was observed to be up-regulated (Fig. 3G-I). The aberrant expression of genes implicated in both lipid metabolism and the ERS process has been demonstrated to result in lipid accumulation in zebrafish embryos.

The digital images obtained using Oil-Red-O staining revealed that the zebrafish embryos exhibited a greater accumulation of lipids ( Fig. 4A, B). Further examination of blood biochemistry revealed an improvement in the levels of lipids, including triglycerides (TG), total cholesterol (TCHO), eicosapentaenoic acid (EPA) and docosahexaenoic acid (DHA), were improved (Fig. 4C-F). Furthermore, TEM imaging of CDs-treated cells revealed alterations in the morphology of specific organelles (Supplementary Material S10). In comparison, healthy cells that had not undergone the CDs treatment exhibited a slight depression of the nucleus, accompanied by a blurred nuclear membrane and sparse chromatin. The mitochondria (M) exhibited morphological alterations, including swelling and enlargement. In some instances, the matrix within the membrane was dissolved, the membranes were damaged and the cristae disappeared. Additionally, the swelling and membrane damage phenomena also occurred in the rough endoplasmic reticulum (RER). These observations support the discovery of lipid metabolism disorder and ER stress, as evidenced by the results of RNA-Seq.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of CDs on endoplasmic reticulum stress in zebrafish embryos
ce:section.ce:para: In previous experiments, it was demonstrated that CDs induce ER stress, which in turn activates the unfolded protein response (UPR). It has been demonstrated that the UPR signalling pathway plays a role in the precise regulation of cellular metabolism and energy homeostasis (Hetz and Papa, 2018; Wozny et al., 2023). The effects of CDs on the endoplasmic reticulum of zebrafish embryos cardiomyocytes were further investigated by examining the effects of CDs on three UPR signalling pathways. The mRNA levels of grp78, xbp1, ern2, atf6, perk, atf4 and chop, factors associated with three UPR pathways, were detected using RT-qPCR. The levels of grp78 mRNA were found to be 1.72 and 5.36-fold higher, respectively, in the 130 μg/mL and 260 μg/mL groups ( Fig. 5A). The mRNA levels of xbp1 in the 260 μg/mL group were 2.95-fold higher (Fig. 5B). The levels of IRE1 mRNA were observed to increase by 42.13 %, 52.17 %, and 53.27 % in the three dose groups, in comparison to the control group (Fig. 5C). No significant difference was observed in the mRNA levels of atf6 in the 60 μg/mL and 130 μg/mL groups in comparison to the control group. However, in the 260 μg/mL group, a 3.02-fold increase was noted (Fig. 5D). The mRNA levels of perk in the 130 μg/mL and 260 μg/mL groups increased to 16.16 and 18.75-fold higher respectively (Fig. 5E). The mRNA levels of atf4 in the 130 μg/mL and 260 μg/mL groups were observed to be 1.80 and 7.51-fold higher, respectively (Fig. 5F). The mRNA levels of chop at concentrations of 60 μg/mL, 130 μg/mL and 260 μg/mL increased to 1.36-fold, 1.3-fold and 3.55-fold respectively, in comparison with the control group (Fig. 5G). The above results indicated that CDs could potentially induce alterations in the expression levels of genes associated with the unfolded protein response, which in turn may lead to the induction of ER stress.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The CDs induce developmental toxicity in zebrafish embryos through the induction of endoplasmic reticulum stress
ce:section.ce:para: It has been reported that long-term endoplasmic reticulum stress can cause cell damage (Ren et al., 2021) and further lead to toxicity in zebrafish (Su et al., 2018). The results indicated that CDs exposure could induce ER stress. To investigate the relationship between CDs-induced developmental toxicity and ER stress in zebrafish embryos, 4-PBA (an ER stress inhibitor) was added. 4-PBA has been demonstrated to stabilize protein conformation, enhance ER folding capacity and facilitate the appropriate trafficking of mutant proteins (Ibrahim et al., 2011). No significant statistical difference was observed in the mortality rate of zebrafish between the 4-PBA and control groups. In comparison to the control group, the mortality rate of zebrafish embryos in the CDs group increased to 16.67-fold that observed in the control group, whereas the mortality rate in the CDs+4-PBA group recovered to the control level ( Fig. 6A). The effect of CDs on the hatchability of zebrafish at 72 hpf was found to be insignificant(Fig. 6B). Fig. 6C illustrates that there was no significant difference in blood flow velocity between the 4-PBA and control groups. The blood flow velocity of zebrafish embryos in the CDs group exhibited a 48.8 % reduction, whereas that of the CDs+4-PBA group demonstrated a 57.76 % recovery compared to the control. No significant difference was observed in heart rate between the 4-PBA group and the control group. In comparison to the control group, the heart rate of zebrafish embryos in the CDs group exhibited a 17.29 % decline, whereas in the CDs+4-PBA group, a 5.26 % recovery was observed (Fig. 6D). No statistically significant difference was observed in the incidence of cardiac malformations between the 4-PBA group and the control group. In contrast, the prevalence of cardiac malformations in zebrafish embryos exposed to CDs was found to be 24 times higher than in the control group, while the incidence in the CDs+4-PBA group was comparable to that of the control group (Fig. 6E). No significant difference was observed in the SV-BA distance between the 4-PBA group and the control group. However, the SV-BA distance of zebrafish embryos in the CDs group exhibited an increase of 28.83 %, while that in the CDs+4-PBA group demonstrated a decrease of 22.65 % (Fig. 6F).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The CDs induce lipid metabolism disorder of zebrafish embryos through endoplasmic reticulum stress
ce:section.ce:para: Our previous research findings clearly demonstrated that CDs could induce lipid accumulation and ER stress. To further investigate the relationship between ER stress and lipid metabolism disorder, 4-PBA was added. The combined treatment of CDs and 4-PBA at 260 μg/mL led to a significant reduction in TG and TCHO levels ( Fig. 7A-B), and a restoration of ER stress-related gene expression (Fig. 7C-I). The above results clearly demonstrate that ER stress is the cause of the lipid metabolism disorder caused by CDs.

The particle diameter of the carbon dots, prepared by electrochemical stripping, was confirmed to be within the range of 2.48 ± 0.44 nm by morphological characterisation. The abundant hydrophilic functional groups on the CD surface permitted effective dispersion in the experimental medium. Their average hydrodynamic diameter was 19.67 ± 0.36 nm as obtained from a dynamic light scattering particle sizer., exhibiting excellent colloidal stability. The UV spectra demonstrated that the surface of the carbon dots was abundant in carboxyl and hydroxyl groups, which provided structural evidence that the carbon dots exhibited excellent water solubility. These results collectively substantiated that the CDs synthesised in our laboratory are constituted of a graphitic structure with minimal crystallisation, which is ascribed to the generation of oxygen-containing groups.

CDs have the potential to be utilized in a number of biomedical applications, including bioimaging and drug delivery, due to their distinctive structural and photophysical characteristics. It is therefore evident that a comprehensive investigation of the potential nanotoxicity of CDs in vivo is only necessary to facilitate the safe and effective application of CDs in practice. The photoluminescent properties of CDs were employed to assess their biodistribution in zebrafish. Our results demonstrate that CDs exhibit high permeability and were capable of crossing a range of physiological barriers, accumulating in numerous tissues, including the brain, yolk sac, liver, and gastrointestinal tract. Related reports have also indicated that CDs are predominantly distributed in organs such as the heart, liver, kidney, lung, and spleen, which is consistent with the findings of our study (Liao et al., 2021; Truskewycz et al., 2022). Additionally, it was demonstrated that CDs induced pericardial and yolk sac oedema, spinal curvature, growth retardation and mortality in zebrafish (Umar et al., 2023). This finding is also consistent with the developmental toxicity phenotype in zebrafish caused by CDs observed in our study, thus further establishing that CDs were capable of affecting cardiac development in zebrafish larvae.

Histological lipid chemistry studies have demonstrated that exposure to CDs results in the deposition of lipids throughout the body of zebrafish, including the liver, brain, yolk sac and hearing. This accumulation is characterised by the formation of lipid droplets in Oil Red O stains (Chen et al., 2024; Yang et al., 2021). The results of our comprehensive investigation revealed that carbon spot exposure resulted in elevated cholesterol and triglyceride levels in zebrafish. Additionally, oil red O staining demonstrated an accumulation of lipids in the yolk sac, liver, and heart regions of zebrafish following carbon spot exposure. Concomitantly, the levels of TCHO and TG, the principal constituents of lipids, were markedly elevated. The synthesis and metabolism of these compounds have a profound impact on lipid metabolism, and their quantitative analysis is fundamental for the assessment of lipid metabolism disorders in zebrafish (Yang et al., 2018; Le Bras, 2021). These findings collectively indicate that CDs may induce aberrant lipid metabolism in zebrafish. Disorders of lipid metabolism have been demonstrated to induce growth retardation and developmental abnormalities in zebrafish (Moran, 2007b; Qian et al., 2019), as evidenced by yolk sac malformations and pericardial oedema (Teng et al., 2019b). It can thus be inferred that the mechanism by which CDs cause developmental toxicity may be related to disorders of lipid metabolism.

In order to gain insight into the mechanism of CDs-induced lipid accumulation in zebrafish, we conducted a transcriptomic analysis of the zebrafish exposed to CDs using high-throughput sequencing. GO and KEGG enrichment analyses revealed that several lipid metabolism-related pathways or biological processes were affected by CDs at the transcriptional level, thereby demonstrating the complexity of the causes of CDs-induced lipid accumulation. Moreover, by integrating the results of qRT-PCR and RNA sequencing with the elevated total cholesterol (TCHO) and triglyceride (TG) levels observed, we postulated that CDs affect lipid metabolism in the following ways: there was an increase in fatty acid synthesis. As illustrated in Fig. 2D, to CDs resulted in the up-regulation of elovl1a and elovl7a mRNA, which in turn enhanced the synthesis of monounsaturated fatty acids and contributed to TG deposition in zebrafish. TCHO is a multifunctional molecule that plays a pivotal role in the maintenance of normal cellular structure and intercellular signalling (Simons and Toomre, 2000). Reports consistent with the present study demonstrates that CDs exposure increases mRNA levels of cyp51, a key enzyme in cholesterol synthesis, and decreases mRNA levels of cyp7a1, which plays a pivotal role as a rate-limiting enzyme in this process (Qi et al., 2015). The oxidation of fatty acids serves to mitigate the toxicity associated with lipid accumulation by metabolising excess free fatty acids. Acyl-CoA oxidase 1 (ACOX1) represents a rate-limiting enzyme in this process, with peroxisome proliferator-activated receptor (PPAR) involvement in lipid oxidation through the regulation of ACOX1 (Yang et al., 2023a). The results of the present study indicate that CDs were capable of downregulating the mRNA expression of ACOX1 and PPARβ, which may restrict the conversion of fatty acids to acetyl-CoA in the tricarboxylic acid cycle, thereby promoting lipid accumulation. The transcription factor screbf1, which plays a pivotal role in regulating fatty acid synthesis, engages in the process of TG synthesis alongside apgat4. During triglyceride (TG) translocation, TG can be transported from the liver to peripheral tissues with the involvement of apolipoprotein B (ApoB) (Hussain et al., 2003). Collectively, our findings indicate that exposure to CDs results in a notable up-regulation of mRNA expression of screbf1, apgat4, apob, apodb, and apoda2, which stimulates TG synthesis and ultimately leads to TG accumulation in zebrafish embryos (Fig. 3).

The transcriptomic data from the present study indicated that genes associated with endoplasmic reticulum function were also altered, suggesting that CDs may contribute to impaired endoplasmic reticulum function. The endoplasmic reticulum is the primary organelle engaged in lipid metabolism and possesses the capacity to synthesise an array of lipids, including cholesterol, phospholipids and neutral lipids (Lev, 2012). A recent study has demonstrated that CDs can affect skeletal development by inducing an increase in intracellular calcium and activating endoplasmic reticulum stress (Zhu et al., 2022). The results of the quantitative reverse transcription polymerase chain reaction (qRT-PCR) conducted in the present study further demonstrated that the expression of endoplasmic reticulum folding protein response (UPR)-related genes (ern2, xbp1s, and atf4a) was upregulated, suggesting that exposure to CDs may induce endoplasmic reticulum stress (ERS). Endoplasmic reticulum stress (ERS) has been identified as a potential mechanism through which external substances may induce disturbances in lipid metabolism (Basseri and Austin, 2012). For instance, evidence indicates that exposure to cyanobacterial toxins can induce aberrant lipid metabolism in male zebrafish through the mechanism of ERS. Similarly, the use of environmental antifouling agents has been linked to fish toxicity and hepatic lipid accumulation, which also appear to result from ERS induction (Zhang et al., 2020). Consequently, our findings substantiate the hypothesis that CDs can induce lipid metabolism disorders by disrupting the endoplasmic reticulum and inducing endoplasmic reticulum stress, which in turn causes developmental abnormalities in zebrafish organs such as the heart.

In this study, we initially observed that carbon dots (CDs) can result in a reduction in the malformation rate, mortality, and spontaneous locomotor activity in both the male and female subjects. Additionally, the developmental toxicity mechanism of the CDs was identified as the developmental abnormality of zebrafish embryos induced by CDs through the induction of ERS, which subsequently triggers lipid over-accumulation by disrupting lipid metabolism processes. This work offers a novel perspective on the capacity of CDs to induce developmental toxicity following early-life exposure, while also elucidating the underlying mechanism through the lens of ERS-associated dysregulation of lipid metabolism. The findings presented herein serve as a reliable and valuable reference point for nanomaterials with early life toxicity. This will provide a robust foundation for the safe and appropriate use of nanomaterials in the future.

The study was supported by the Undergraduate Training for Program Innovation and Entrepreneurship, Soochow University (No. 202310285071Z) and the Opening Fund of NHC Key Laboratory of Chronobiology (Sichuan) (No. NHCC-2024-02).

The study was also supported by the Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Preventive and Translational Medicine for Geriatric Diseases, MOE Key Laboratory of Geriatric Diseases and Immunology; Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (PAPD) and Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Preventive and Translational Medicine for Major Chronic Non-communicable Diseases

Xiaoyan Zhong: Writing – original draft. Liwen Zeng: Writing – review & editing. Ying Zhu: Data curation. Yunnuo Cai: Methodology. Ouxi Shen: Supervision. Qiyao Ma: Validation, Investigation. Jie Zhang: Conceptualization. Xiaoyao Song: Supervision, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.ecoenv.2024.117361.
